A **town** is a type of a [human settlement](/wiki/Human_settlement "Human settlement"), generally larger than a [village](/wiki/Village "Village") but smaller than a [city](/wiki/City "City").[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1)

The criteria for distinguishing a town vary globally, often depending on factors such as population size, economic character, administrative status, or historical significance. In some regions, towns are formally defined by legal charters or government designations, while in others, the term is used informally. Towns typically feature centralized services, infrastructure, and governance, such as [municipal authorities](/wiki/Municipal_authorities "Municipal authorities"), and serve as hubs for commerce, education, and cultural activities within their regions.

The concept of a town varies culturally and legally. For example, in the United Kingdom, a town may historically derive its status from a [market town](/wiki/Market_town "Market town") designation or [royal charter](/wiki/City_status_in_the_United_Kingdom "City status in the United Kingdom"), while in the United States, the term is often loosely applied to incorporated [municipalities](/wiki/Municipality "Municipality"). In some countries, such as Australia and Canada, distinctions between towns, cities, and rural areas are based on population thresholds. Globally, towns play diverse roles, ranging from agricultural service centers to suburban communities within metropolitan areas.

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The word "town" shares an origin with the German word *[Zaun](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Zaun#German "wikt:Zaun")* ("fence"), the Dutch word *[tuin](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/tuin#Dutch "wikt:tuin")* ("garden, yard; fence, enclosure"), and the [Old Norse](/wiki/Old_Norse "Old Norse") *[tún](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/t%C3%BAn#Old_Norse "wikt:tún")* ("enclosure, as for a homestead").[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2) The original [Proto-Germanic](/wiki/Proto-Germanic_language "Proto-Germanic language") word, *[\*tūną](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Reconstruction:Proto-Germanic/t%C5%ABn%C4%85#Proto-Germanic "wikt:Reconstruction:Proto-Germanic/tūną")*, is thought to be an early borrowing from *[\*dūnom](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Reconstruction:Proto-Celtic/d%C5%ABnom#Proto-Celtic "wikt:Reconstruction:Proto-Celtic/dūnom")* (cf. [Old Irish](/wiki/Old_Irish "Old Irish") *[dún](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/d%C3%BAn#Old_Irish "wikt:dún")*, Welsh *[din](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/din#Welsh "wikt:din")*).[[3]](#cite_note-Town-3)

The original sense of the word in both Germanic and Celtic was that of a fortress or an enclosure. Cognates of the English word *town* in many modern Germanic languages designate a "fence" or a "hedge".[[3]](#cite_note-Town-3) In English and Dutch, the meaning of the word took on the sense of the space which these fences enclosed, and through which a track must run.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In England, a "town" was a small community that could not afford or was not allowed to build walls or other larger fortifications, and built a [palisade](/wiki/Palisade "Palisade") or stockade instead.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In the Netherlands, this space was a garden, more specifically those of the wealthy, which had a high fence or a wall around them (like the garden of the palace of [Het Loo](/wiki/Het_Loo_Palace "Het Loo Palace") in [Apeldoorn](/wiki/Apeldoorn "Apeldoorn"), which was the model for the privy garden of [William III](/wiki/William_III_of_England "William III of England") and [Mary II](/wiki/Mary_II_of_England "Mary II of England") at [Hampton Court](/wiki/Hampton_Court "Hampton Court")). In Old Norse *tún* means a (grassy) place between farmhouses, and the word is still used with a similar meaning in modern Norwegian.

Old English *[tūn](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/tun#Old_English "wikt:tun")* became a common place-name suffix in England and southeastern Scotland during the Anglo-Saxon settlement period. In [Old English](/wiki/Old_English "Old English") and Early and Middle [Scots](/wiki/Scots_language "Scots language"), the words *ton*, *toun*, etc. could refer to diverse kinds of settlements from agricultural estates and holdings, partly picking up the Norse sense (as in the Scots word *[fermtoun](/wiki/Fermtoun "Fermtoun")*) at one end of the scale, to fortified municipalities.[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1) Other common Anglo-Saxon suffixes included *ham* 'home', *stede* 'stead', and *burh* 'bury, borough, burgh'.

In [toponymic](/wiki/Toponymic "Toponymic") terminology, names of individual towns and cities are called *astyonyms* or *astionyms* (from [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek") ἄστυ 'town, city', and ὄνομα 'name').[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoom199613-4)

## Meaning

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Meaning")]

In some cases, *town* is an alternative name for "city" or "village" (especially a small city or large village; and occasionally even [hamlets](/wiki/Hamlet_(place) "Hamlet (place)")). Sometimes, the word *town* is short for *[township](/wiki/Township "Township")*. In general, today towns can be differentiated from townships, villages, or hamlets on the basis of their economic character, in that most of a town's population will tend to derive their living from manufacturing industry, commerce, and public services rather than [primary sector](/wiki/Primary_sector "Primary sector") industries such as agriculture or related activities.

A place's population size is not a reliable determinant of urban character. In many areas of the world, e.g. in India at least until recent times, a large village might contain several times as many people as a small town. In the United Kingdom, there are historical cities [that are far smaller](/wiki/List_of_urban_areas_in_England_by_population "List of urban areas in England by population") than the larger towns.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/Moisakula_-_panoramio_%281%29.jpg/250px-Moisakula_-_panoramio_%281%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Moisakula_-_panoramio_(1).jpg)

[Mõisaküla](/wiki/M%C3%B5isak%C3%BCla "Mõisaküla") is a small town in the southern part of [Estonia](/wiki/Estonia "Estonia"), just next to the border of [Latvia](/wiki/Latvia "Latvia"). The town's current population is less than 1,000 inhabitants.

The modern phenomenon of extensive [suburban](/wiki/Suburb "Suburb") growth, satellite urban development, and migration of city dwellers to villages has further complicated the definition of towns, creating communities urban in their economic and cultural characteristics but lacking other characteristics of urban localities.

Some forms of non-rural settlement, such as temporary mining locations, may be clearly non-rural, but have at best a questionable claim to be called a town.

Towns often exist as distinct governmental units, with legally defined borders and some or all of the appurtenances of local government (e.g. a police force). In the United States these are referred to as "incorporated towns". In other cases the town lacks its own governance and is said to be "unincorporated". The existence of an unincorporated town may be legally set out by other means, e.g. zoning districts. In the case of some [planned communities](/wiki/Planned_community "Planned community"), the town exists legally in the form of covenants on the properties within the town. The [United States census](/wiki/United_States_census "United States census") identifies many [census-designated places](/wiki/Census-designated_place "Census-designated place") (CDPs) by the names of unincorporated towns which lie within them; however, those CDPs typically include rural and suburban areas and even surrounding villages and other towns.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/View_of_Mariehamn%2C_2022.jpg/250px-View_of_Mariehamn%2C_2022.jpg)](/wiki/File:View_of_Mariehamn,_2022.jpg)

Aerial view of [Mariehamn](/wiki/Mariehamn "Mariehamn"), the town in [Åland](/wiki/%C3%85land "Åland") with over 10,000 inhabitants

The distinction between a town and a city similarly depends on the approach: a city may strictly be an administrative entity which has been granted that designation by law, but in informal usage, the term is also used to denote an urban locality of a particular size or importance: whereas a [medieval](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") city may have possessed as few as 10,000 inhabitants, today some[*[who?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] consider an urban place of fewer than 100,000 as a town, even though there are many officially designated cities that are much smaller than that.

Starting in March 2021, the then-193 [member states of the United Nations](/wiki/Member_states_of_the_United_Nations "Member states of the United Nations") have been involved in an effort led by EU aparatusses to agree on a common statistical definition of cities, towns and rural areas.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

### Age of towns scheme

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Age of towns scheme")]

Australian geographer [Thomas Griffith Taylor](/wiki/Thomas_Griffith_Taylor "Thomas Griffith Taylor") proposed a classification of towns based on their age and pattern of [land use](/wiki/Land_use "Land use"). He identified five types of towns:[[6]](#cite_note-6)

* *Infantile towns*, with no clear [zoning](/wiki/Zoning "Zoning")
* *Juvenile towns*, which have developed an area of [shops](/wiki/Retailing "Retailing")
* *Adolescent towns*, where factories have started to appear
* *Early mature towns*, with a separate area of [high-class](/wiki/Social_class "Social class") housing
* *Mature towns*, with defined industrial, commercial and various types of residential area

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: History")]

Through different periods of recorded history, many towns have grown into sizeable settlements, with the development of properties, centres of culture, and specialized economies.

### Neolithic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Neolithic")]

[Çatalhöyük](/wiki/%C3%87atalh%C3%B6y%C3%BCk "Çatalhöyük"), currently an archaeological site, was considered to be the oldest inhabited town, or [proto-city](/wiki/Proto-city "Proto-city"), that existed from around 7100 BC.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Inscribed as a [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site"), it remains a depopulated town with a complex of ruins.

In [Roman times](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome"), a villa was a rural settlement formed by a main residential building and another series of secondary buildings. It constituted the center from which an agricultural holding was administered. These are sometimes referred to as *[villa rustica](/wiki/Villa_rustica "Villa rustica")* (country villa), which consist of modest farmhouses.[[8]](#cite_note-8) With the consolidation of large estates during the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire"), the town became the center of large farms.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

A distinction was created between rustic and urban settlements:

* Country villas (*villa rustica*),[[9]](#cite_note-:2-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) from where the exploitation of resources was directed, slave workers resided, livestock were kept and production was stored.
* Urban villas (*pars urbana)*,[[9]](#cite_note-:2-9) in which the [lord](/wiki/Lord "Lord") resided and which increasingly adopted the architectural and beautification forms typical of urban mansions. When from the first century the great territorial property was divided between the area directly exploited by the lord and that ceded to tenant settlers, urban villas became the centers of the administrative power of the lords,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] appearing the forms of vassalage typical of feudalism of the fourth century.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## By country

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: By country")]

### Afghanistan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Afghanistan")]

Further information: [List of cities in Afghanistan](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Afghanistan "List of cities in Afghanistan")

In [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan"), a [city](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Afghanistan "List of cities in Afghanistan") and a town are both referred to as *shār* ([Dari](/wiki/Dari_language "Dari language"): شهر; [Pashto](/wiki/Pashto_language "Pashto language"): ښار).[[11]](#cite_note-11) The capital of each of its 34 [provinces](/wiki/Provinces_of_Afghanistan "Provinces of Afghanistan") may include a major city such as [Kabul](/wiki/Kabul "Kabul") whose population is over five million people or a town such as [Parun](/wiki/Parun "Parun"), the capital of [Nuristan Province](/wiki/Nuristan_Province "Nuristan Province"), whose population is less than 20,000 people.

### Albania

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Albania")]

In [Albania](/wiki/Albania "Albania") *qytezë* means 'town', which is very similar to the word for city (*qytet*), although there is no official use of the term for any settlement.
In Albanian, *qytezë* means 'small city' or 'new city', while in ancient times it referred to a small residential center within the walls of a castle.

### Australia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Australia")]

In Australia, most rural and regional centres of population can be called towns; many small towns have populations of less than 200.[[12]](#cite_note-12) The smallest may be described as townships.

In addition, some local government entities are officially styled as towns in Queensland, [South Australia](/wiki/South_Australia "South Australia"), Western Australia and the [Northern Territory](/wiki/Northern_Territory "Northern Territory"), and formerly also (till the 1990s) in [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria_(state) "Victoria (state)").

### Austria

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Austria")]

See also: [List of cities and towns in Austria](/wiki/List_of_cities_and_towns_in_Austria "List of cities and towns in Austria")

The Austrian legal system does not distinguish between villages, towns, and cities. The country is partitioned into 2098 municipalities ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Gemeinden*) of fundamentally equal rank. Larger municipalities are designated as market towns ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Marktgemeinden*) or cities (*Städte*), but these distinctions are purely symbolic and do not confer additional legal responsibilities. There is a number of smaller communities that are labelled cities because they used to be regional population centers in the distant past. The city of [Rattenberg](/wiki/Rattenberg "Rattenberg") for example has about 400 inhabitants. The city of [Hardegg](/wiki/Hardegg "Hardegg") has about 1200 inhabitants.

There are no unincorporated areas.

Of the 201 cities in Austria, 15 are [statutory cities](/wiki/Statutory_city_(Austria) "Statutory city (Austria)") (*Statutarstädte*). A statutory city is a city that is vested, in addition to its purview as a municipality, with the duties of a [district administrative authority](/wiki/District_(Austria) "District (Austria)"). The status does not come with any additional autonomy: district administrative authorities are essentially just service centers that citizens use to interact with the national government, for example to apply for driver licenses or passports. The national government generally uses the provinces to run these points of contact on its behalf; in the case of statutory cities, the municipality gets to step up.

### Brazil

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Brazil")]

In Brazil, since 1938, it was defined that the seat of the municipalities would pass to the category of city and give it the name and the districts would be designated by the name of their respective seats, and if they were not municipal seats, they would have the category of town.

### Bulgaria

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Bulgaria")]

Main article: [List of cities and towns in Bulgaria](/wiki/List_of_cities_and_towns_in_Bulgaria "List of cities and towns in Bulgaria")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Peshtera--downtown.jpg/250px-Peshtera--downtown.jpg)](/wiki/File:Peshtera--downtown.jpg)

The town of [Peshtera](/wiki/Peshtera "Peshtera"), [Bulgaria](/wiki/Bulgaria "Bulgaria")

Bulgarians do not, in general, differentiate between 'city' and 'town'. However, in everyday language and media the terms "large towns" and "small towns" are in use. "Large towns" usually refers to [Sofia](/wiki/Sofia "Sofia"), [Plovdiv](/wiki/Plovdiv "Plovdiv"), [Varna](/wiki/Varna,_Bulgaria "Varna, Bulgaria") and [Burgas](/wiki/Burgas "Burgas"), which have population over 200,000. [Ruse](/wiki/Ruse,_Bulgaria "Ruse, Bulgaria") and [Stara Zagora](/wiki/Stara_Zagora "Stara Zagora") are often included as well due to presence of relatively developed infrastructure and population over 100,000 threshold. It is difficult to call the remaining [provincial capitals](/wiki/Provinces_of_Bulgaria "Provinces of Bulgaria") "large towns" as, in general, they are less developed and have shrinking population, some with as few as 30,000 inhabitants.

In Bulgaria the [Council of Ministers](/wiki/Council_of_Ministers_of_Bulgaria "Council of Ministers of Bulgaria") defines what constitutes a settlement, while the [President of Bulgaria](/wiki/President_of_Bulgaria "President of Bulgaria") grants each settlement its title. In 2005 the requirement that villages that wish to classify themselves as town must have a social and technical infrastructure, as well as a population of no fewer than 3500 people. For resort settlements the requirements are lower with the population needing to be no fewer than 1000 people but infrastructure requirements remain.

### Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Canada")]

Main articles: [List of towns in Canada](/wiki/List_of_towns_in_Canada "List of towns in Canada") and [Municipal government in Canada](/wiki/Municipal_government_in_Canada "Municipal government in Canada")

The legal definition of a town in Canada varies by [province or territory](/wiki/Provinces_and_territories_of_Canada "Provinces and territories of Canada"), as each has jurisdiction over defining and legislating towns, cities and other types of municipal organization within its own boundaries.

The province of [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec") is unique in that it makes no distinction under law between towns and cities. There is no intermediate level in French between *village* and *ville* (*municipality* is an administrative term usually applied to a legal, not geographical entity), so both are combined under the single legal status of *[ville](/wiki/Ville "Ville")*. While an informal preference may exist among English speakers as to whether any individual *ville* is commonly referred to as a city or as a town, no distinction and no objective legal criteria exist to make such a distinction under law.

[Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario") allows municipalities to select whichever administrative term they like with no legal distinction existing between towns, townships, cities, and villages.[[13]](#cite_note-13) Instead all municipalities, with the exception of Toronto and Ottawa, fall into one of three legal categories under the Municipalities Act: Single-tier (I.e. towns that are located within a region or county but that are considered separate for municipal purposes such as [Hamilton](/wiki/Hamilton,_Ontario "Hamilton, Ontario")), lower-tier (i.e. municipalities that are part of a region or county such as [St. Catharines](/wiki/St._Catharines "St. Catharines")), or upper-tier (i.e. regional municipalities such as [Niagara](/wiki/Regional_Municipality_of_Niagara "Regional Municipality of Niagara")).[[14]](#cite_note-14) Accordingly, many larger municipalities continue to use the title of town due to it better reflecting the character of the municipality. For example, [Oakville](/wiki/Oakville,_Ontario "Oakville, Ontario") (2021 Population: 213,759) is the largest municipality to use the title of town to reflect its largely suburban character while other municipalities such as [Richmond Hill](/wiki/Richmond_Hill,_Ontario "Richmond Hill, Ontario") (2021 Population: 202,022) have opted to change their status from "town" to "city" to encourage investment.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

### Chile

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Chile")]

In [Chile](/wiki/Chile "Chile"), towns (Spanish: *pueblos*) are defined by the National Statistics Institute (INE) as an urban entity with a population from 2001 to 5000 or an area with a population from 1001 to 2000 and an established economic activity.

### Czechia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Czechia")]

Main articles: [Obec](/wiki/Obec "Obec") and [List of cities and towns in the Czech Republic](/wiki/List_of_cities_and_towns_in_the_Czech_Republic "List of cities and towns in the Czech Republic")

In [Czechia](/wiki/Czech_Republic "Czech Republic"), a municipality can obtain the title of a [city](/wiki/Statutory_city_(Czech_Republic) "Statutory city (Czech Republic)") ([Czech](/wiki/Czech_language "Czech language"): *statutární město*), town ([Czech](/wiki/Czech_language "Czech language"): *město*) or [market town](/wiki/M%C4%9Bstys "Městys") ([Czech](/wiki/Czech_language "Czech language"): *městys*). The title is granted by law.

Statutory cities (in English usually called just "cities"), which are defined by law no. 128/2000 Coll.,[[16]](#cite_note-16) can define their own self-governing municipal districts. There are 26 such cities, in addition to [Prague](/wiki/Prague "Prague"), which is a *de facto* statutory city. All the Czech municipalities with more than 40,000 inhabitants are cities.

Town and market town are above all ceremonious honorary degrees, referring to population, history and regional significance of a municipality. [As the statistics of Czech municipalities](/wiki/Obec#Statistics "Obec") shows, towns usually have between 1,000 and 35,000 inhabitants, with median around 4,000 and average around 6,500. Nowadays a municipality must have at least 3,000 inhabitants to have the right to request the town title. Market towns usually have between 500 and 4,000 inhabitants, with median and average both around 1,000.

### Denmark

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Denmark")]

In Denmark, in many contexts no distinction is made between "city", "town" and "village"; all three translate as *by*. In more specific use, for small villages and [hamlets](/wiki/Hamlet_(place) "Hamlet (place)") the word *landsby* (meaning 'country town') is used, while the Danish equivalent of English *city* is *storby* (meaning 'large town'). For formal purposes, [urban areas](/wiki/Urban_area "Urban area") having at least 200 inhabitants are considered *by*.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

Historically some towns held various privileges, the most important of which was the right to hold market. They were administered separately from the rural areas in both fiscal, military and legal matters. Such towns are known as *købstad* (roughly the same meaning as *borough* albeit deriving from a different etymology) and they retain the exclusive right to the title even after the last vestiges of their privileges vanished through the reform of the local administration carried through in 1970.

### Estonia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Estonia")]

In [Estonia](/wiki/Estonia "Estonia"), there is no distinction between a town and a city as the word *linn* is used for both bigger and smaller settlements, which are bigger than villages and boroughs. There are 30 [municipal towns](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Estonia "Municipalities of Estonia") (*omavalitsuslik linn*) in Estonia and a [further 17 towns](/wiki/List_of_cities_and_towns_in_Estonia "List of cities and towns in Estonia"), which have merged with a municipal parish (*vallasisene linn*).

### Finland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Finland")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/Vammala%2C_Onkiniemenkatu.jpg/250px-Vammala%2C_Onkiniemenkatu.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vammala,_Onkiniemenkatu.jpg)

The town of [Sastamala](/wiki/Sastamala "Sastamala"), Finland

In Finland, there is no distinction between a town and a city as the word *kaupunki* is used for both bigger and smaller settlements, which are bigger than villages and boroughs; although when talking about the word *town*, the word *pikkukaupunki* is used (*pikku* means 'little' or 'small'). There are over one hundred [municipal towns](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Finland "Municipalities of Finland") in Finland.

### France

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: France")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Salins_les_Bains_2.JPG/250px-Salins_les_Bains_2.JPG)](/wiki/File:Salins_les_Bains_2.JPG)

The town of [Salins-les-Bains](/wiki/Salins-les-Bains "Salins-les-Bains"), France

From an administrative standpoint, the smallest level of local authorities are all called [*communes*](/wiki/Communes_of_France "Communes of France"). They can have anywhere from a handful to millions of inhabitants, and France has 36,000 of them. The French term for *town* is *[bourg](/w/index.php?title=Bourg_(town)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bourg (town) (page does not exist)")[[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bourg "fr:Bourg")]*[[18]](#cite_note-18) but French laws generally do not distinguish between towns and cities which are all commonly called *villes*. However, some laws do treat these authorities differently based on the population and different rules apply to the three big cities Paris, [Lyon](/wiki/Lyon "Lyon") and [Marseille](/wiki/Marseille "Marseille"). For historical reasons, [six communes](/wiki/List_of_French_villages_destroyed_in_World_War_I "List of French villages destroyed in World War I") in the [Meuse](/wiki/Meuse_(department) "Meuse (department)") département exist as independent administrative entities despite having no inhabitants at all.

For statistical purposes, the national statistical institute ([INSEE](/wiki/INSEE "INSEE")) operates a distinction between urban areas with fewer than 2,000 inhabitants and bigger communes, the latter being called *villes*. Smaller settlements are usually called *villages*.

### Germany

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Germany")]

Main article: [List of cities and towns in Germany](/wiki/List_of_cities_and_towns_in_Germany "List of cities and towns in Germany")

"Großstadt" redirects here. For the painting, see [*Metropolis* (Dix)](/wiki/Metropolis_(Dix) "Metropolis (Dix)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Putbus_%282011-05-21%29_14.JPG/250px-Putbus_%282011-05-21%29_14.JPG)](/wiki/File:Putbus_(2011-05-21)_14.JPG)

[Putbus](/wiki/Putbus "Putbus") on [Rügen Island](/wiki/R%C3%BCgen "Rügen"), Germany

Germans do not, in general, differentiate between 'city' and 'town'. The German word for both is *Stadt*, as it is the case in many other languages that do not differentiate between these concepts. The word for a 'village', as a smaller settlement, is *Dorf*. However, the International Statistics Conference of 1887 defined different sizes of *Stadt*, based on their population size, as follows: *Landstadt* ('country town'; under 5,000), *Kleinstadt* ('small town'; 5,000 to 20,000), *Mittelstadt* ('middle town'; between 20,000 and 100,000) and *Großstadt* ("large town"; 100,000 to 1,000,000).[[19]](#cite_note-19) The term *Großstadt* may be translated as 'city'. In addition, Germans may speak of a *Millionenstadt*, a city with anywhere between one and five million inhabitants (such as [Cologne](/wiki/Cologne "Cologne"), [Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich"), [Hamburg](/wiki/Hamburg "Hamburg") and Berlin). Also, a city with more than five million inhabitants is often referred to as a *Megastadt* (commonly translated as [megacity](/wiki/Megacity "Megacity")).[[20]](#cite_note-20)

Historically, many settlements became a *Stadt* by being awarded a [*Stadtrecht*](/wiki/Town_privileges "Town privileges") in medieval times. In modern German language use, the historical importance, the existence of central functions (education, retail etc.) and the population density of an urban place might also be taken as characteristics of a *Stadt*. The modern local government organisation is subject to the laws of each [state](/wiki/States_of_Germany "States of Germany") and refers to a *Gemeinde* ([municipality](/wiki/States_of_Germany#Municipalities_(Gemeinden) "States of Germany")), regardless of its historic title. While most *Gemeinden* form part of a *Landkreis* ([district](/wiki/States_of_Germany#Administrative_districts_(Kreise) "States of Germany")) on a higher tier of local government, larger towns and cities may have the status of a *kreisfreie Stadt*, combining both the powers of a municipality and a district.

Designations in different states are as diverse as e.g. in Australian States and Territories, and differ from state to state. In some German states, the words *Markt* ('market'), *Marktflecken* (both used in southern Germany) or *Flecken* ('spot'; northern Germany e.g. in [Lower Saxony](/wiki/Lower_Saxony "Lower Saxony")) designate a town-like residential community between *Gemeinde* and *Stadt* with special importance to its outer conurbation area. Historically those had *Marktrecht* (market right) but not full town privileges; see [Market town](/wiki/Market_town "Market town"). The legal denomination of a specific settlement may differ from its common designation (e.g. *Samtgemeinde* – a legal term in [Lower Saxony](/wiki/Lower_Saxony "Lower Saxony") for a group of villages [*Dorf*, pl. *Dörfer*] with common local government created by combining municipalities [*Gemeinde*, pl. *Gemeinden*]).

### Greece and Cyprus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Greece and Cyprus")]

In ordinary speech, Greeks use the word χωριό ('village') to refer to smaller settlements and the word πόλη or πολιτεία ('city') to refer to larger ones. Careful speakers may also use the word κωμόπολη to refer to towns with a population of 2,000–9,999.

In Greek administrative law there used to be a distinction between δήμοι, i.e. municipalities with more than 10,000 inhabitants or considered important for some other geographical (county seats), historical or ecclesiastical (bishops' seats) reason, and κοινότητες, referring to smaller self-governing units, mostly villages. A sweeping reform, carried out in two stages early in the 21st century, merged most κοινότητες with the nearest δήμοι, dividing the whole country into 325 self-governing δήμοι. The former municipalities survive as administrative subdivisions (δημοτικά διαμερίσματα, δημοτικές ενότητες).

Cyprus, including the Turkish-occupied areas, is also divided into 39 δήμοι (in principle, with at least 5,000 inhabitants, though there are exceptions) and 576 κοινότητες.

### Hong Kong

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Hong Kong")]

Main article: [List of cities and towns in Hong Kong](/wiki/List_of_cities_and_towns_in_Hong_Kong#Towns "List of cities and towns in Hong Kong")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/ShatinTownHall_20070529.jpg/250px-ShatinTownHall_20070529.jpg)](/wiki/File:ShatinTownHall_20070529.jpg)

Nearly every town in Hong Kong has its own town hall. The picture shows the [Sha Tin Town Hall](/wiki/Sha_Tin_Town_Hall "Sha Tin Town Hall") in the town of [Sha Tin](/wiki/Sha_Tin "Sha Tin").

Hong Kong started developing new towns in the 1950s, to accommodate exponential population increase. The first new towns included [Tsuen Wan](/wiki/Tsuen_Wan_New_Town "Tsuen Wan New Town") and [Kwun Tong](/wiki/Kwun_Tong "Kwun Tong"). In the late 1960s and the 1970s, another stage of new town developments was launched. Nine new towns have been developed so far. Land use is carefully planned and development provides plenty of room for public housing projects. Rail transport is usually available at a later stage. The first towns are [Sha Tin](/wiki/Sha_Tin_New_Town "Sha Tin New Town"), [Tsuen Wan](/wiki/Tsuen_Wan_New_Town "Tsuen Wan New Town"), [Tuen Mun](/wiki/Tuen_Mun_New_Town "Tuen Mun New Town") and [Tseung Kwan O](/wiki/Tseung_Kwan_O "Tseung Kwan O"). [Tuen Mun](/wiki/Tuen_Mun_New_Town "Tuen Mun New Town") was intended to be self-reliant, but was not successful and turned into a [bedroom community](/wiki/Bedroom_community "Bedroom community") like the other new towns. More recent developments are [Tin Shui Wai](/wiki/Tin_Shui_Wai_New_Town "Tin Shui Wai New Town") and [North Lantau (Tung Chung-Tai Ho)](/wiki/North_Lantau_New_Town "North Lantau New Town").

### Hungary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Hungary")]

In Hungary there is no official distinction between a city and a town (the word for both in Hungarian is *város*). Nevertheless, the expressions formed by adding the adjectives *kis* ('small') and *nagy* ('large') to the beginning of the root word (e.g. *nagyváros*) have been normalized to differentiate between cities and towns (towns being smaller, therefore bearing the name *kisváros*.) In Hungary, a village can gain the status of *város* ('town'), if it meets a set of diverse conditions for quality of life and development of certain public services and utilities (e.g. having a local secondary school or installing full-area sewage collection pipe network). Every year the Minister of Internal Affairs selects candidates from a committee-screened list of applicants, whom the President of Republic usually affirms by issuing a bill of town's rank to them. Since being a town carries extra fiscal support from the government, many relatively small villages try to win the status of *városi rang* ('town rank') nowadays.

Before the [fall of communism](/wiki/End_of_communism_in_Hungary "End of communism in Hungary") in 1990, Hungarian villages with fewer than 10,000 residents were not allowed to become towns. Recently some settlements as small as 2,500 people have received the rank of town (e.g. Visegrád, Zalakaros or Gönc) and meeting the conditions of development is often disregarded to quickly elevate larger villages into towns. As of middle 2013, there are 346 towns in Hungary, encompassing some 69% of the entire population.

Towns of more than 50,000 people are able to gain the status of *megyei jogú város* (town with the rights of a [county](/wiki/County "County")), which allows them to maintain a higher degree of [services](/wiki/Public_service "Public service"). (There are a few exceptions, when towns of fewer than 50,000 people gained the status: [Érd](/wiki/%C3%89rd "Érd"), [Hódmezővásárhely](/wiki/H%C3%B3dmez%C5%91v%C3%A1s%C3%A1rhely "Hódmezővásárhely"), [Salgótarján](/wiki/Salg%C3%B3tarj%C3%A1n "Salgótarján") and [Szekszárd](/wiki/Szeksz%C3%A1rd "Szekszárd"))[[21]](#cite_note-21) As of middle 2013, there are only 23 such towns in Hungary.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

### India

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: India")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/One_of_the_high_streets_in_Paravur.jpg/250px-One_of_the_high_streets_in_Paravur.jpg)](/wiki/File:One_of_the_high_streets_in_Paravur.jpg)

A street in [Paravur](/wiki/Paravur,_Kollam "Paravur, Kollam") town, India

The [2011 Census of India](/wiki/2011_Census_of_India "2011 Census of India") defines towns of two types: statutory town and [census town](/wiki/Census_town "Census town"). Statutory town is defined as all places with a [municipality](/wiki/Municipality "Municipality"), [corporation](/wiki/Municipal_corporation "Municipal corporation"), [cantonment board](/wiki/Cantonment_Board "Cantonment Board") or notified town area committee. Census towns are defined as places that satisfy the following criteria:

1. Minimum population of 5,000
2. At least 75% of male working population engaged in non-agricultural pursuits
3. Density of population at least 400/km2. (1,000 per sq. mile).

All the statutory towns, census towns and [out growths](/wiki/Out_growth "Out growth") are considered as urban settlements, as opposed to rural areas.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

Towns in India usually have basic infrastructure like shops, electricity, bitumenised roads, [post offices](/wiki/Post_offices "Post offices"), banks, telephone facilities, high schools and sometimes a few government offices. The human population living in these towns may be a few thousand. There are some towns which can be labelled as Main road town.

In state of [Karnataka](/wiki/Karnataka "Karnataka"), towns are known as *Pete* or *Pura* in the [Kannada language](/wiki/Kannada_language "Kannada language"). Sometimes the terms *pattana* ('city') or *ooru*, which generally means 'place', are used for towns. The administrative council which governs these towns is known as *Pura Sabhe* or *Nagara Sabhe* in Kannada depending on the number of people living within the town's boundaries.

### Iran

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Iran")]

In contemporary [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language") texts, no distinction is made between *city* and *town*; both translate as *Shahr* (شهر). In older Persian texts (until the first half of the 20th century), the Arabic word *Qasabeh* (قصبه) was used for a town. However, in the past 50 years,[*[as of?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] this word has become obsolete.

There is a word in Persian which is used for special sort of satellite townships and city neighborhoods. It is *Shahrak* (شهرک), (lit.: 'small city').
Another smaller type of town or neighborhood in a big city is called *Kuy* (کوی). *Shahrak* and *Kuy* each have different legal definitions.
Large cities such as [Tehran](/wiki/Tehran "Tehran"), [Mashhad](/wiki/Mashhad "Mashhad"), [Isfahan](/wiki/Isfahan "Isfahan"), [Tabriz](/wiki/Tabriz "Tabriz"), etc. which have millions inhabitants are referred to as *Kalan-shahr* (کلان‌شهر), [metropole](/wiki/Metropole "Metropole").

The pace in which different large villages have gained city status in [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran") shows a dramatic increase in the last two decades.

Bigger cities and towns usually are centers of a [township](/wiki/Township "Township") (in Persian: *Shahrestan* (شهرستان). *Shahrestan* itself is a subdivision of *Ostan* (استان), 'province'.

### Iraq

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Iraq")]

The word *Jarayeh* (جرَية) is used to describe villages, the word *Garmat* (كَرمة) to describe towns, and the word *Wilaya* (ولاية) to describe cities.

### Ireland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Ireland")]

Main article: [List of populated places in the Republic of Ireland](/wiki/List_of_populated_places_in_the_Republic_of_Ireland "List of populated places in the Republic of Ireland")

The **Local Government act 2001** provides that from 1 January 2002 (section 10 subsection (3)):

> Within the county in which they are situated and of which they form part, there continue to be such other local government areas as are set out in Schedule 6 which –
>
> * (a) in the case of the areas set out in Chapter 1 of Part 1 of that Schedule, shall be known as boroughs, and
> * (b) in the case of the areas set out in Chapter 2 of Part 1 and Part 2 of that Schedule,
>
> shall be known as towns, and in this Act a reference to a town shall include a reference to a borough.

These provisions affect the replacement of the boroughs, towns and urban districts which existed before then. Similar reforms in the nomenclature of local authorities (but not their functions) are affected by section 11 part 17 of the act includes provision (section 185(2))

> Qualified electors of a town having a population of at least 7,500 as ascertained at the last preceding census or such other figure as the Minister may from time to time prescribe by regulations, and not having a [town council](/wiki/Town_council "Town council"), may make a proposal in accordance with paragraph (b) for the establishment of such a council

and contains provisions enabling the establishment of new town councils and provisions enabling the dissolution of existing or new town councils in certain circumstances

The reference to "town having a population of at least 7,500 as ascertained at the last preceding census" hands much of the power relating to defining what is in fact a town over to the Central Statistics Office and their criteria are published as part of each census.

#### Planning and Development Act 2000

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Planning and Development Act 2000")]

Another reference to the Census and its role in determining what is or is not a town for some administrative purpose is in the Planning and Development act 2000 (part II chapter I which provides for Local area plans):

> A local area plan shall be made in respect of an area which—
>
> * (i) is designated as a town in the most recent census of population, other than a town designated as a suburb or environs in that census,
> * (ii) has a population in excess of 2,000, and
> * (iii) is situated within the functional area of a planning authority which is a county council.

#### Central Statistics Office criteria

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Central Statistics Office criteria")]

These are set out in full at [2006 Census Appendices](https://www.cso.ie/en/media/csoie/census/documents/census_2006_Appendices.pdf).

In short they speak of "towns with legally defined boundaries" (i.e. those established by the Local Government Act 2001) and the remaining 664 as "census towns", defined by themselves since 1971 as "a cluster of 50 or more occupied dwellings in which within a distance of 800 meters there is a nucleus of 30 occupied houses on both sides of the road or twenty occupied [Houses](/wiki/House "House") on one side of the road". There is also a "200 meter criterion" for determining whether a house is part of a census town.

### Isle of Man

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Isle of Man")]

There are four settlements which are historically and officially designated as towns (Douglas, Ramsey, Peel, Castletown); however

* Peel is also sometimes referred to as a city by virtue of its cathedral.
* Onchan and Port Erin are both larger in population than the smallest "town", having expanded in modern times, but are designated as villages.

### Israel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Israel")]

[Modern Hebrew](/wiki/Modern_Hebrew "Modern Hebrew") does provide a word for the concept of a town: *Ayara* (עיירה), derived from *Ir* (עיר), the biblical word for 'city'. However, the term *ayara* is normally used only to describe towns in foreign countries, i.e. urban areas of limited population, particularly when the speaker is attempting to evoke nostalgic or romantic attitudes. The term is also used to describe a [shtetl](/wiki/Shtetl "Shtetl"), a pre-[Holocaust](/wiki/The_Holocaust "The Holocaust") Eastern European Jewish town.

Within Israel, established urban areas are always referred to as cities (with one notable exception explained below) regardless of their actual size. Israeli law does not define any nomenclature for distinction between urban areas based on size or any other factor – meaning that all urban settlements in Israel are legally referred to as "cities".

The exception to the above is the term *Ayeret Pituakh* (עיירת פיתוח, lit. 'Development Town') which is applied to certain cities in Israel based on the reasons for their establishment. These cities, created during the earlier decades of Israeli independence (1950s and 1960s, generally), were designed primarily to serve as commercial and transportation hubs, connecting smaller agricultural settlements in the northern and southern regions of the country (the "Periphery") to the major urban areas of the coastal and central regions. Some of these development towns have since grown to a comparatively large size, and yet are still referred to as development towns, particularly when the speaker wishes to emphasize their (often low) socio-economic status. Nonetheless, they are rarely (if ever) referred to simply as towns; when referring to one directly, it will be called either a development town or a city, depending on context.

### Italy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Italy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Satriano_di_Lucania_centro.JPG/250px-Satriano_di_Lucania_centro.JPG)](/wiki/File:Satriano_di_Lucania_centro.JPG)

[Satriano di Lucania](/wiki/Satriano_di_Lucania "Satriano di Lucania"), a town in the [Melandro Valley](/w/index.php?title=Melandro&action=edit&redlink=1 "Melandro (page does not exist)"), [Basilicata](/wiki/Basilicata "Basilicata"), south Italy

Although Italian provides different words for city (*città*), town (*cittadina or paese*) and village (*villaggio*, old-fashioned, or *[frazione](/wiki/Frazione "Frazione")*, most common), no legal definitions exist as to how settlements must be classified. Administratively, both towns and cities are ruled as [comuni](/wiki/Comuni "Comuni")/[comunes](/wiki/Comune "Comune"), while villages might be subdivisions of the former.
Generally, in everyday speech, a town is larger or more populated than a village and smaller than a city. Various cities and towns together may form a metropolitan area (*area metropolitana*). A city can also be a culturally, economically, or politically prominent community with respect to surrounding towns. Moreover, a city can be such by [Presidential](/wiki/President_of_Italy "President of Italy") decree. A town, in contrast, can be an inhabited place which would elsewhere be styled a city, but has not received any official recognition.
Remarkable exceptions do exist: for instance, [Bassano del Grappa](/wiki/Bassano_del_Grappa "Bassano del Grappa"), was given the status of *città* in 1760 by [Francesco Loredan](/wiki/Francesco_Loredan "Francesco Loredan")'s [dogal](/wiki/Doge_(title) "Doge (title)") decree and has since then carried this title. Also, the Italian word for 'town' (*paese* with lowercase P) must not be confused with the Italian word for 'country/nation' (*Paese* usually with uppercase P).

### Japan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Japan")]

In Japan, city status (市 *[shi](/wiki/Cities_of_Japan "Cities of Japan")*) was traditionally reserved for only a few particularly large settlements. Over time however the necessary conditions to be a city have been watered down and today the only loose rules that apply are having a population over 50,000 and over 60% of the population in a "city centre". In recent times many small villages and towns have merged in order to form a city despite seeming geographically to be just a collection of villages.

The distinction between towns (町 *[machi/chō](/wiki/Towns_of_Japan "Towns of Japan")*) and villages (村 *[mura/son](/wiki/Villages_of_Japan "Villages of Japan")*) is largely unwritten and purely one of population size when the settlement was founded with villages having under 10,000 and towns 10,000–50,000.

### Korea

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Korea")]

Main article: [Eup (administrative division)](/wiki/Eup_(administrative_division) "Eup (administrative division)")

In both of South Korea and North Korea, towns are called *[eup](/wiki/Eup_(administrative_division) "Eup (administrative division)")* (읍). Most cities in North Korea are built around a central square. Perhaps it is to symbolize the importance of the society over the individual, or just a handy place for mass gatherings and celebrations.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

### Latvia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Latvia")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Valka%2C_Semin%C4%81ra_iela_%281%29.JPG/250px-Valka%2C_Semin%C4%81ra_iela_%281%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Valka,_Semin%C4%81ra_iela_(1).JPG)

The town of [Valka](/wiki/Valka "Valka"), [Latvia](/wiki/Latvia "Latvia")

In [Latvia](/wiki/Latvia "Latvia"), towns and cities are indiscriminately called *pilsēta* in singular form. The name is a [contraction](/wiki/Contraction_(grammar) "Contraction (grammar)") of two Latvian words: *pils* ('castle') and *sēta* ('fence'), making it very obvious what is meant by the word – what is situated between the castle and the castle fence. However, a city can be called *lielpilsēta* (big city) or *mazpilsēta* (small city/town) in reference to its size. Latvia also has administrative units such as state cities([valstspilsēta](/w/index.php?title=Valstspils%C4%93ta&action=edit&redlink=1 "Valstspilsēta (page does not exist)")[[lv](https://lv.wikipedia.org/wiki/Valstspils%C4%93ta "lv:Valstspilsēta")]) A village is called *ciemats* or *ciems* in Latvian.

### Lithuania

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Lithuania")]

Main article: [List of towns in Lithuania](/wiki/List_of_towns_in_Lithuania "List of towns in Lithuania")

In [Lithuanian](/wiki/Lithuania "Lithuania"), a city is called *[miestas](/wiki/Miestas "Miestas")[[lt](https://lt.wikipedia.org/wiki/miestas "lt:miestas")]* and a town is called *[miestelis](/wiki/Miestelis "Miestelis")[[lt](https://lt.wikipedia.org/wiki/miestelis "lt:miestelis")]* (literally 'small *miestas*'). [Metropolises](/wiki/Metropolis "Metropolis") are called [*didmiestis*](/w/index.php?title=Didmiestis&action=edit&redlink=1 "Didmiestis (page does not exist)")[[lt](https://lt.wikipedia.org/wiki/didmiestis "lt:didmiestis")] (literally 'big *miestas*').

### Malaysia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Malaysia")]

In [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia"), a town ([Malay](/wiki/Malay_language "Malay language"): *bandar* or *kota*) is the area administered by a municipal council (*Majlis Perbandaran*).

### New Zealand

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: New Zealand")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
