In [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand"), the term "town" has no current statutory meaning. Certain towns are regionally part of cities.

### Netherlands

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Netherlands")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/41012_Zuidhavenpoort_zierikzee_Met_Noordhavenpoort.jpg/250px-41012_Zuidhavenpoort_zierikzee_Met_Noordhavenpoort.jpg)](/wiki/File:41012_Zuidhavenpoort_zierikzee_Met_Noordhavenpoort.jpg)

The small town of [Zierikzee](/wiki/Zierikzee "Zierikzee"), Netherlands

Before 1848 there was a legal distinction between *stad* and non-*stad* parts of the country, but the word no longer has any legal significance. About 220 places were granted *stadsrechten* ('city rights') and are still so called for historical and traditional reasons, though the word is also used for large urban areas that never obtained such rights. Because of this, in the Netherlands, no distinction is made between *city* and *town*; both translate as *stad*. A hamlet (*gehucht*) usually has fewer than 1,000 inhabitants, a village (*dorp*) ranges from 1,000 up to 25,000 inhabitants, and a place above 25,000 can call itself either village or city, mostly depending on historic reasons or size of the place. As an example, [The Hague](/wiki/The_Hague "The Hague") never gained city rights, but because of its size – more than half a million inhabitants – it is regarded as a city. [Staverden](/wiki/Staverden "Staverden"), with only 40 inhabitants, would be a hamlet, but because of its city rights it may call itself a city.

For statistical purposes, the Netherlands has three sorts of cities:

* *kleine stad* (small city): 50,000–99,999 inhabitants
* *middelgrote stad* (medium-sized city): 100,000–249,999 inhabitants
* *grote stad* (large city): 250,000 or more

Only [Amsterdam](/wiki/Amsterdam "Amsterdam"), [Rotterdam](/wiki/Rotterdam "Rotterdam"), [The Hague](/wiki/The_Hague "The Hague") and [Utrecht](/wiki/Utrecht "Utrecht") are regarded as a *grote stad*.

### Norway

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Norway")]

In Norway, *city* and *town* both translate to [*by*](/wiki/By_(city_or_town) "By (city or town)"), even if a city may be referred to as *storby* ('large town'). They are all part of and administered as a *[kommune](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Norway "Municipalities of Norway")* ('[municipality](/wiki/Municipality "Municipality")').

Norway has had inland the northernmost city in the world: Hammerfest.[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26) Now the record is held by New Ålesund on the Norwegian island Svalbard.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Brygga_i_T%C3%B8nsberg.JPG/250px-Brygga_i_T%C3%B8nsberg.JPG)](/wiki/File:Brygga_i_T%C3%B8nsberg.JPG)

The oldest town in Norway is [Tønsberg](/wiki/T%C3%B8nsberg "Tønsberg"). Originally a [market town](/wiki/Market_town "Market town"), it is today a big city.

The oldest town in Norway is [Tønsberg](/wiki/T%C3%B8nsberg "Tønsberg"), founded during the [Viking Age](/wiki/Viking_Age "Viking Age"). The year when the town was founded and which person who founded it is unknown, but [Snorri Sturluson](/wiki/Snorri_Sturluson "Snorri Sturluson") says in the [Saga of Harald Fairhair](/wiki/Saga_of_Harald_Fairhair "Saga of Harald Fairhair") that the [market town](/wiki/Market_town "Market town") existed before the [Battle of Hafrsfjord](/wiki/Battle_of_Hafrsfjord "Battle of Hafrsfjord") in the year 872. Nowadays Tønsberg is considered a city (*storby*).[[28]](#cite_note-28)

### Philippines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Philippines")]

Main articles: [List of cities and municipalities in the Philippines](/wiki/List_of_cities_and_municipalities_in_the_Philippines "List of cities and municipalities in the Philippines") and [Municipalities of the Philippines](/wiki/Municipalities_of_the_Philippines "Municipalities of the Philippines")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/Loboc_Bohol_1.jpg/250px-Loboc_Bohol_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Loboc_Bohol_1.jpg)

The town center of [Loboc, Bohol](/wiki/Loboc,_Bohol "Loboc, Bohol")

In the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"), the local official equivalent of the town is the [municipality](/wiki/Philippine_municipality "Philippine municipality") (Filipino: *bayan*). Every municipality, or town, in the country has a mayor (*alkalde*) and a vice mayor (*bise alkalde*) as well as local town officials ([Sangguniang Bayan](/wiki/Sangguniang_Bayan "Sangguniang Bayan")). Philippine towns, otherwise called municipalities, are composed of a number of villages and communities called *[barangays](/wiki/Barangays "Barangays")* with one (or a few cluster of) *barangay*(s) serving as the town center or *[poblacion](/wiki/Poblacion "Poblacion")*.

Unique in Philippine towns is that they have fixed budget, population and land requirements to become as such, i.e. from a *barangay*, or a cluster of such, to a town, or to become cities, i.e. from town to a city. Respectively, examples of these are the town of [B. E. Dujali](/wiki/Braulio_E._Dujali,_Davao_del_Norte "Braulio E. Dujali, Davao del Norte") in [Davao del Norte](/wiki/Davao_del_Norte "Davao del Norte") province, which was formed in 1998 from a cluster of five *barangays*, and the city of [El Salvador](/wiki/El_Salvador,_Misamis_Oriental "El Salvador, Misamis Oriental"), which was converted from a town to a city in 2007. Each town in the Philippines was classified by its [annual income and budget](/wiki/Municipalities_of_the_Philippines#Income_classification "Municipalities of the Philippines").

A sharp, hierarchical distinction exists between Philippine [cities](/wiki/Philippine_city "Philippine city") (*lungsod* or *siyudad*) and towns, as towns in the country are juridically separate from cities, which are typically larger and more populous (some smaller and less populated) and which political and economic status are above those of towns. This was further supported and indicated by the income classification system implemented by the National Department of Finance, to which both cities and towns fell into their respective categories that indicate they are such as stated under Philippine law. However, both towns and cities equally share the status as [local government units](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_the_Philippines#Local_government_units "Administrative divisions of the Philippines") (LGUs) grouped under and belong to [provinces](/wiki/Provinces_of_the_Philippines "Provinces of the Philippines") and [regions](/wiki/Regions_of_the_Philippines "Regions of the Philippines"); both each are composed of *barangays* and are governed by a mayor and a vice mayor supplemented by their respective LGU legislative councils. However, despite this some towns in the Philippines are significantly larger than some cities in the Philippines such as [Rodriguez, Rizal](/wiki/Rodriguez,_Rizal "Rodriguez, Rizal"), [Santa Maria, Bulacan](/wiki/Santa_Maria,_Bulacan "Santa Maria, Bulacan") and [Minglanilla, Cebu](/wiki/Minglanilla,_Cebu "Minglanilla, Cebu") are actually bigger than some regional centers.

### Poland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Poland")]

Main article: [List of cities and towns in Poland](/wiki/List_of_cities_and_towns_in_Poland "List of cities and towns in Poland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Zamo%C5%9B%C4%87._Ratusz..jpg/250px-Zamo%C5%9B%C4%87._Ratusz..jpg)](/wiki/File:Zamo%C5%9B%C4%87._Ratusz..jpg)

[Zamość](/wiki/Zamo%C5%9B%C4%87 "Zamość") in Poland is an example of a [utopian](/wiki/Utopia "Utopia") [ideal town](/wiki/Ideal_town "Ideal town"). It was declared a [UNESCO World Heritage Site](/wiki/UNESCO_World_Heritage_Site "UNESCO World Heritage Site") in 1992.

In the Polish language, there is no linguistic distinction between a city and a town, both translated *miasto*. The word for both is *miasto*, as a form of settlement distinct from types of rural settlements: village (*wieś*), *[przysiółek](/wiki/Przysi%C3%B3%C5%82ek "Przysiółek")*, **osada**, or **kolonia**, see [Classification of localities and their parts in Poland](/wiki/Classification_of_localities_and_their_parts_in_Poland "Classification of localities and their parts in Poland"). Cities are the biggest municipalities, distinguished through being managed by a city mayor (*prezydent miasta*, literally translated city president) instead of a town mayor (*burmistrz*) as the head of the city executive, thus being informally called *miasto prezydenckie*, with such privilege automatically awarded to municipalities either inhabited by more than 100,000 residents (currently 37) or those enjoying the status of a [city with powiat rights](/wiki/City_with_powiat_rights "City with powiat rights") (currently 66). As of 2022, all of the former group fit into the latter, though it was not always the case in the past. There is, however, a number of exemptions due to historic or political reasons, when a municipality meets neither of these two conditions but nevertheless has the city status, including the only 3 capitals of the former [voivodeships of Poland (1975–1998)](/wiki/Voivodeships_of_Poland_(1975%E2%80%931998) "Voivodeships of Poland (1975–1998)") not meeting the abovementioned criteria, as well as further 38 municipalities which do not fit into any of the mentioned categories but have nevertheless been allowed to keep the earlier awarded status due to unspecified historical reasons. Towns may sometimes be called *miasteczko*, a diminutive colloquially used for localities with a few thousand residents. Such localities have a town mayor (*burmistrz*) as the head of the town executive.

Town/city rights are conferred by government legislation; new towns/cities are designated by the government in an annual regulation effective from the first day of the year. Some settlements tend to remain villages even though they have a larger population than many smaller towns, primarily in order not to lose eligibility for the [European Agricultural Fund for Rural Development](/wiki/European_Agricultural_Fund_for_Rural_Development "European Agricultural Fund for Rural Development"). As of 30 April 2022, there are altogether 2477 municipalities ([gmina](/wiki/Gmina "Gmina")) in Poland, including 1513 rural gminas, while the remaining 968 ones contain cities and towns. Among them, 666 towns are part of an urban-rural gmina while 302 cities and towns are standalone as an urban gmina. The latter group includes 107 cities (governed by a *prezydent miasta*), including 66 [cities with powiat rights](/wiki/City_with_powiat_rights "City with powiat rights"). 37 cities among the latter group are over 100,000, including 18 cities serving as a seat for [voivode](/wiki/Voivode "Voivode") or [voivodeship sejmik](/wiki/Voivodeship_sejmik "Voivodeship sejmik"), informally called voivodeship cities.

### Portugal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Portugal")]

Like other Latin cultures, in Portugal a town (*vila*) is a populated place larger than a village (*aldeia* and smaller than a city (*cidade*). Similarly, although these places are not defined under the [Portuguese Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Portugal "Constitution of Portugal") and have no political and administrative functions (with associated organs), they are defined by law,[[29]](#cite_note-29) and a town must have:

* at least 3,000 voters
* at least half of these services: health unit, pharmacy, cultural centre, public transportation network, post office, commercial food and drinking establishments, primary school and/or bank office

In special cases, villages can receive the status of town if they possess historical, cultural or architectonic importance.

A Portuguese town or city is so merely an urban settlement located in the area of a [municipality](/wiki/Concelho "Concelho"), in comparison to the North American context, where they have political functions.

A town can be enterily located inside the area of a single [freguesia](/wiki/Freguesia "Freguesia") (subdivion of a municipality) or can occupy several freguesias.

[Portuguese local governments heraldry](/wiki/Portuguese_heraldry#Municipal_heraldry "Portuguese heraldry") reflects if the seat of the respective freguesia or municipality is a city, town or another type of settlement. The [coat of arms](/wiki/Coat_of_arms "Coat of arms") of a local government with a seat in a town bears a mural crown with four towers, while the coat of arms of a local government with a set in a city bears a crown with five towers.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

This difference between towns and cities is still in use in other Portuguese-speaking countries. In Brazil, since the beginning of the 20th century, all municipal seats receive the status of city.

### Romania

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Romania")]

Main article: [List of cities and towns in Romania](/wiki/List_of_cities_and_towns_in_Romania "List of cities and towns in Romania")

In Romania there is no official distinction between a city and a town (the word for both in Romanian is *oraş*). Cities and towns in Romania can have the status either of *oraş [municipiu](/wiki/Municipiu "Municipiu")*, conferred to large urban areas, or only *oraş* to smaller urban localities. Some settlements remain villages (*[comune](/wiki/Communes_of_Romania "Communes of Romania")*) even though they have a larger population than other smaller towns.

### Russia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Russia")]

Main article: [Types of inhabited localities in Russia](/wiki/Types_of_inhabited_localities_in_Russia "Types of inhabited localities in Russia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/Vyborg_SevernyVal3-5_006_8242.jpg/250px-Vyborg_SevernyVal3-5_006_8242.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vyborg_SevernyVal3-5_006_8242.jpg)

The town of [Vyborg](/wiki/Vyborg "Vyborg") in [Leningrad Oblast](/wiki/Leningrad_Oblast "Leningrad Oblast"), Russia

Unlike English, the Russian language does not distinguish the terms *city* and *town*—both are translated as город (*gorod*). Occasionally the term город is applied to [urban-type settlements](/wiki/Urban-type_settlement "Urban-type settlement") as well, even though the status of those is not the same as that of a city/town proper.

In Russia, the criteria an inhabited locality needs to meet in order to be granted city/town (*gorod*) status vary in different [federal subjects](/wiki/Federal_subjects_of_Russia "Federal subjects of Russia"). In general, to qualify for this status, an inhabited locality should have more than 12,000 inhabitants and the occupation of no less than 85% of inhabitants must be other than agriculture. However, inhabited localities which were previously granted the city/town status but no longer meet the criteria can still retain the status for historical reasons.

### Singapore

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Singapore")]

Main article: [New towns of Singapore](/wiki/New_towns_of_Singapore "New towns of Singapore")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/1_2014_panorama_bishan_park_aerial_gopro_dji_phantom.jpg/330px-1_2014_panorama_bishan_park_aerial_gopro_dji_phantom.jpg)](/wiki/File:1_2014_panorama_bishan_park_aerial_gopro_dji_phantom.jpg)

[Bishan](/wiki/Bishan,_Singapore "Bishan, Singapore"), one of Singapore's towns, is the 38th biggest in terms of geographical size and the 21st most populated planning area in the country.

In Singapore, towns are large-scale satellite housing developments which are designed to be self-contained. It includes public housing units, a town centre and other amenities.[[31]](#cite_note-31) Helmed by a hierarchy of commercial developments, ranging from a town centre to precinct-level outlets, there is no need to venture out of town to meet the most common needs of residences. Employment can be found in industrial estates located within several towns. Educational, health care, and recreational needs are also taken care of with the provision of schools, hospitals, parks, sports complexes, and so on. The most populous town in the country is [Bedok](/wiki/Bedok "Bedok").

### South Africa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: South Africa")]

In South Africa the [Afrikaans](/wiki/Afrikaans "Afrikaans") term *dorp* is used interchangeably with the English equivalent *town*. A town is a settlement that has a size that is smaller than that of a city.

### Spain

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=50 "Edit section: Spain")]

In Spain, the equivalent of town would be *villa*, a population unit between a village (*pueblo*) and a city (*ciudad*), and is not defined by the number of inhabitants, but by some historical rights and privileges dating from the Middle Ages, such as the right to hold a market or fair. For instance, while [Madrid](/wiki/Madrid "Madrid") is technically a *villa*, [Barcelona](/wiki/Barcelona "Barcelona"), with a smaller population, is known as a city.

### Sweden

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=51 "Edit section: Sweden")]

See also: [Stad (Sweden)](/wiki/Stad_(Sweden) "Stad (Sweden)") and [Köping](/wiki/K%C3%B6ping "Köping")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/%C3%96vre_Finngr%C3%A4nd_7_Sta_Maria_29_Visby_Gotland.jpg/250px-%C3%96vre_Finngr%C3%A4nd_7_Sta_Maria_29_Visby_Gotland.jpg)](/wiki/File:%C3%96vre_Finngr%C3%A4nd_7_Sta_Maria_29_Visby_Gotland.jpg)

View towards [St Mary's Cathedral](/wiki/Visby_Cathedral "Visby Cathedral") in [Visby](/wiki/Visby "Visby"), Sweden. Visby is one of the most well-preserved former [Hanseatic](/wiki/Hanseatic_League "Hanseatic League") cities in Sweden and a [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site"). Today it is the seat of [Gotland Municipality](/wiki/Gotland_Municipality "Gotland Municipality").

The Swedish language does not differentiate between towns and cities in the English sense of the words; both words are commonly translated as *stad*, a term which has no legal significance today. The term *tätort* is used for an [urban area](/wiki/Urban_areas_in_Sweden "Urban areas in Sweden") or a locality, which however is a statistical rather than an administrative concept and encompasses densely settled villages with only 200 inhabitants as well as the major cities. The word *[köping](/wiki/K%C3%B6ping "Köping")* corresponds to an English *[market town](/wiki/Market_town "Market town")* *(chipping)* or German *[Markt](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Markt "wikt:Markt")* but is mainly of historical significance, as the term is not used today and only survives in some [toponyms](/wiki/Toponym "Toponym"). Some towns with names ending in *-köping* are cities with over 100,000 inhabitants today, e.g. [Linköping](/wiki/Link%C3%B6ping "Linköping").

Before 1971, 132 larger [municipalities](/wiki/Municipality "Municipality") in Sweden enjoyed special royal charters as *[stad](/wiki/Stad_(Sweden) "Stad (Sweden)")* instead of *[kommun](/wiki/Kommun "Kommun")* (which is similar to a US [county](/wiki/County_(United_States) "County (United States)")). However, since 1971 all municipalities are officially defined as *kommun*, thus making no legal difference between, for instance, [Stockholm](/wiki/Stockholm "Stockholm") and a small countryside municipality. Every urban area that was a *stad* before 1971 is still often referred to as a *stad* in daily speech. Since the 1980s, 14 of these municipalities have branded themselves as *stad* again, although this has no legal or administrative significance, as they still have to refer to themselves as *kommun* in all legal documentation.

For statistical purposes, [Statistics Sweden](/wiki/Statistics_Sweden "Statistics Sweden") officially defines a *stad* as an urban area of at least 10,000 inhabitants. Since 2017 it also defines a *storstad* (literally 'big town') as a municipality with a population of at least 200,000 of which at least 200,000 are in its largest *tätort*.[[32]](#cite_note-32) This means that [Stockholm](/wiki/Stockholm "Stockholm"), [Gothenburg](/wiki/Gothenburg "Gothenburg") and [Malmö](/wiki/Malm%C3%B6 "Malmö") are *storstäder*, i.e. 'major cities', while [Uppsala](/wiki/Uppsala "Uppsala"), with a population of approximately 230,000 in the municipality, which covers an unusually large area, almost three times larger than the combined land area of the municipalities of Stockholm, Gothenburg and Malmö, is not. The largest contiguous urban area within Uppsala municipality has a population of well below 200,000, while the population of both Malmö municipality, with a land area only 1/14 the size of Uppsala municipality, and Malmö *tätort*, i.e. contiguous urban area, is well over 300,000, and the population of the [Malmö Metropolitan Area](/wiki/Metropolitan_Malm%C3%B6 "Metropolitan Malmö"), with a land area only slightly larger than Uppsala Municipality, is well over 700,000. A difference in the size and population of the urban area between Uppsala and the smallest *storstad* in Sweden, Malmö, is the reason Statistics Sweden changed the definition of *storstad* in 2017.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

### Ukraine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=52 "Edit section: Ukraine")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/%D0%91%D0%BE%D0%B3%D0%BE%D1%80%D0%BE%D0%B4%D1%87%D0%B0%D0%BD%D0%B8%2C_4_%D0%B4%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%B6%D0%B0%D0%B2%D0%BD%D0%B0_%D0%BF%D0%BE%D0%B6%D0%B5%D0%B6%D0%BD%D0%BE-%D1%80%D1%8F%D1%82%D1%83%D0%B2%D0%B0%D0%BB%D1%8C%D0%BD%D0%B0_%D1%87%D0%B0%D1%81%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%BD%D0%B0_%D1%83_%D0%94%D0%A1%D0%9D%D0%A1_%D0%A3%D0%BA%D1%80%D0%B0%D1%97%D0%BD%D0%B8%2C_2.jpg/250px-%D0%91%D0%BE%D0%B3%D0%BE%D1%80%D0%BE%D0%B4%D1%87%D0%B0%D0%BD%D0%B8%2C_4_%D0%B4%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%B6%D0%B0%D0%B2%D0%BD%D0%B0_%D0%BF%D0%BE%D0%B6%D0%B5%D0%B6%D0%BD%D0%BE-%D1%80%D1%8F%D1%82%D1%83%D0%B2%D0%B0%D0%BB%D1%8C%D0%BD%D0%B0_%D1%87%D0%B0%D1%81%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%BD%D0%B0_%D1%83_%D0%94%D0%A1%D0%9D%D0%A1_%D0%A3%D0%BA%D1%80%D0%B0%D1%97%D0%BD%D0%B8%2C_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%91%D0%BE%D0%B3%D0%BE%D1%80%D0%BE%D0%B4%D1%87%D0%B0%D0%BD%D0%B8,_4_%D0%B4%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%B6%D0%B0%D0%B2%D0%BD%D0%B0_%D0%BF%D0%BE%D0%B6%D0%B5%D0%B6%D0%BD%D0%BE-%D1%80%D1%8F%D1%82%D1%83%D0%B2%D0%B0%D0%BB%D1%8C%D0%BD%D0%B0_%D1%87%D0%B0%D1%81%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%BD%D0%B0_%D1%83_%D0%94%D0%A1%D0%9D%D0%A1_%D0%A3%D0%BA%D1%80%D0%B0%D1%97%D0%BD%D0%B8,_2.jpg)

Fire station in town of [Bohorodchany](/wiki/Bohorodchany "Bohorodchany")

In Ukraine the term *town* (містечко, *mistechko*) existed from the Medieval period until 1925, when it was replaced by the [Soviet government](/wiki/Politics_of_the_Soviet_Union "Politics of the Soviet Union") with *[urban type settlement](/wiki/Urban_type_settlement "Urban type settlement")*.[[34]](#cite_note-34) Historically, a town in the Ukrainian lands was a smaller populated place that was chartered under the [German town law](/wiki/German_town_law "German town law") and had a market square (see [Market town](/wiki/Market_town "Market town")).

### United Kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=53 "Edit section: United Kingdom")]

Main article: [City status in the United Kingdom](/wiki/City_status_in_the_United_Kingdom "City status in the United Kingdom")

#### England and Wales

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=54 "Edit section: England and Wales")]

Main articles: [List of urban areas in England by population](/wiki/List_of_urban_areas_in_England_by_population "List of urban areas in England by population") and [list of urban areas in Wales by population](/wiki/List_of_urban_areas_in_Wales_by_population "List of urban areas in Wales by population")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Rugby_town_centre.jpg/250px-Rugby_town_centre.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rugby_town_centre.jpg)

A traditional English town centre at [Rugby](/wiki/Rugby,_Warwickshire "Rugby, Warwickshire")

In England and Wales, a town traditionally was a settlement which had a charter to hold a [market](/wiki/Market_(place) "Market (place)") or [fair](/wiki/Fair "Fair") and therefore became a "[market town](/wiki/Market_town "Market town")". Market towns were distinguished from villages in that they were the economic hub of a surrounding area, and were usually larger and had more facilities.

In parallel with popular usage, however, there are many technical and official definitions of what constitutes a town, to which various interested parties cling.

In modern official usage the term *town* is employed either for old market towns, or for settlements which have a [town council](/wiki/Town_council "Town council"), or for settlements which elsewhere would be classed a city, but which do not have [the legal right](/wiki/City_status_in_the_United_Kingdom "City status in the United Kingdom") to call themselves such. Any parish council can decide to describe itself as a town council, but this will usually only apply to the smallest "towns" (because larger towns will be larger than a single civil parish).

Not all settlements which are commonly described as towns have a town council or borough council. In fact, because of many successive changes to the structure of local government, there are now few large towns which are represented by a body closely related to their historic borough council. These days, a smaller town will usually be part of a local authority which covers several towns. And where a larger town *is* the seat of a local authority, the authority will usually cover a much wider area than the town itself (either a large rural hinterland, or several other, smaller towns).

Additionally, there are "[new towns](/wiki/New_towns_in_the_United_Kingdom "New towns in the United Kingdom")" which were created during the 20th century, such as [Basildon](/wiki/Basildon "Basildon"), [Redditch](/wiki/Redditch "Redditch") and [Telford](/wiki/Telford "Telford").

Some settlements which describe themselves as towns (e.g. [Shipston-on-Stour](/wiki/Shipston-on-Stour "Shipston-on-Stour"), [Warwickshire](/wiki/Warwickshire "Warwickshire")) are smaller than some large villages (e.g. [Kidlington](/wiki/Kidlington "Kidlington"), [Oxfordshire](/wiki/Oxfordshire "Oxfordshire")).

The status of a *city* is reserved for places that have [letters patent](/wiki/Letters_patent "Letters patent") entitling them to the name, historically associated with the possession of a cathedral. Some large municipalities (such as [Northampton](/wiki/Northampton "Northampton") and [Bournemouth](/wiki/Bournemouth "Bournemouth")) are legally [boroughs](/wiki/Borough "Borough") but not cities, whereas some cities are quite small — such as [Ely](/wiki/Ely,_Cambridgeshire "Ely, Cambridgeshire") or [St David's](/wiki/St_David%27s "St David's"). The city of [Brighton and Hove](/wiki/Brighton_and_Hove "Brighton and Hove") was created from the two former towns and some surrounding villages, and within the city the correct term for the former distinct entities is somewhat unclear.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/7/7e/Windhill.jpg/250px-Windhill.jpg)](/wiki/File:Windhill.jpg)

[Bishop's Stortford](/wiki/Bishop%27s_Stortford "Bishop's Stortford")

It appears that a city may become a town, though perhaps only through administrative error: [Rochester](/wiki/Rochester,_Kent "Rochester, Kent") in Kent had been a city for centuries but, when in 1998 the [Medway](/wiki/Medway "Medway") district was created, a bureaucratic blunder meant that Rochester lost its official [city status](/wiki/City_status_in_the_United_Kingdom "City status in the United Kingdom") and is now technically a town.

It is often thought that towns with [bishops' seats](/wiki/Cathedral "Cathedral") rank automatically as cities: however, [Chelmsford](/wiki/Chelmsford "Chelmsford") was a town until 5 June 2012 despite being the seat of the [diocese of Chelmsford](/wiki/Diocese_of_Chelmsford "Diocese of Chelmsford"), created in 1914. [St Asaph](/wiki/St_Asaph "St Asaph"), which is the seat of the [diocese of St Asaph](/wiki/Diocese_of_St_Asaph "Diocese of St Asaph"), only became a city on 1 June 2012 though the diocese was founded in the mid-sixth century. In reality, the pre-qualification of having a cathedral of the established [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England"), and the formerly established [Church in Wales](/wiki/Church_in_Wales "Church in Wales") or [Church of Ireland](/wiki/Church_of_Ireland "Church of Ireland"), ceased to apply from 1888.

The word *town* can also be used as a general term for urban areas, including cities and in a few cases, districts within cities. In this usage, a city is a type of town; a large one, with a certain status. For example, central [Greater London](/wiki/Greater_London "Greater London") is sometimes referred to colloquially as "London town". (The "[City of London](/wiki/City_of_London "City of London")" is the historical nucleus, informally known as the "Square Mile", and is administratively separate from the rest of Greater London, while the [City of Westminster](/wiki/City_of_Westminster "City of Westminster") is also technically a city and is also a [London borough](/wiki/London_borough "London borough").) [Camden Town](/wiki/Camden_Town "Camden Town") and [Somers Town](/wiki/Somers_Town,_London "Somers Town, London") are districts of London, as [New Town](/wiki/New_Town,_Edinburgh "New Town, Edinburgh") is a district of [Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh "Edinburgh") – actually the Georgian centre.

In recent years the division between cities and towns has grown, leading to the establishment of groups like the Centre for Towns, who work to highlight the issues facing many towns.[[35]](#cite_note-35) Towns also became a significant issue in the [2020 Labour Party leadership election](/wiki/2020_Labour_Party_leadership_election_(UK) "2020 Labour Party leadership election (UK)"), with [Lisa Nandy](/wiki/Lisa_Nandy "Lisa Nandy") making significant reference to Labour needing to win back smaller towns which have swung away from the party.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

#### Scotland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=55 "Edit section: Scotland")]

Main articles: [List of burghs in Scotland](/wiki/List_of_burghs_in_Scotland "List of burghs in Scotland") and [List of towns and cities in Scotland by population](/wiki/List_of_towns_and_cities_in_Scotland_by_population "List of towns and cities in Scotland by population")

In Scotland the word *town* has no specific legal meaning and (especially in areas which were or are still [Gaelic](/wiki/Scottish_Gaelic "Scottish Gaelic")-speaking) can refer to a mere collection of buildings (e.g. a *farm-town* or in [Scots](/wiki/Scots_language "Scots language") *ferm-toun*), not all of which might be inhabited, or to an inhabited area of any size which is not otherwise described in terms such as [city](/wiki/City "City"), [burgh](/wiki/Burgh "Burgh"), etc. Many locations of greatly different size will be encountered with a name ending with *-town*, *-ton*, *-toun* etc. (or beginning with the Gaelic equivalent *baile* etc.).[[37]](#cite_note-37)

"[Burgh](/wiki/Burgh "Burgh")" (pronounced *burruh*) is the [Scots](/wiki/Scots_language "Scots language") term for a town or a municipality. They were highly [autonomous](/wiki/Autonomous_area "Autonomous area") units of [local government](/wiki/Local_government_in_Scotland "Local government in Scotland") from at least the 12th century until their abolition in 1975, when a new [regional structure of local government](/wiki/Local_government_areas_of_Scotland_1973_to_1996 "Local government areas of Scotland 1973 to 1996") was introduced across the country. Usually based upon a town, they had a [municipal corporation](/wiki/Municipal_corporation "Municipal corporation") and certain rights, such as a degree of [self-governance](/wiki/Self-governance "Self-governance") and representation in the sovereign [Parliament of Scotland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Scotland "Parliament of Scotland") adjourned in 1707.

The term no longer describes units of local government, although various claims are made from time to time that the legislation used was not competent to change the status of the Royal Burghs described below. The status is now chiefly ceremonial but various functions have been inherited by current councils (e.g. the application of various endowments providing for public benefit) which might only apply within the area previously served by a burgh; in consequence a burgh can still exist (if only as a defined geographical area) and might still be signed as such by the current local authority. The word 'burgh' is generally not used as a synonym for 'town' or 'city' in everyday speech, but is reserved mostly for government and administrative purposes.

Historically, the most important burghs were [royal burghs](/wiki/Royal_burgh "Royal burgh"), followed by [burghs of regality](/wiki/Burgh_of_regality "Burgh of regality") and [burghs of barony](/wiki/Burgh_of_barony "Burgh of barony"). Some newer settlements were only designated as [police burghs](/wiki/Police_burgh "Police burgh") from the 19th century onward, a classification which also applies to most of the older burghs.

### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=56 "Edit section: United States")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/Wyatt-indiana-from-above.jpg/250px-Wyatt-indiana-from-above.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wyatt-indiana-from-above.jpg)

The tiny farming community of [Wyatt, Indiana](/wiki/Wyatt,_Indiana "Wyatt, Indiana")

In the United States, the meaning of the term town is different in each state. In some states, a town is a town if the state says it is. In other states, like Wisconsin, a town is a subdivision of a county. In other states, like Michigan, the name "town" has no official meaning. People use it to describe any place where many people live. In the six New England states, a town is a kind of municipality that is like a city, but smaller. For example, in Pennsylvania, a town is a specific type of incorporated municipality, distinct from townships and boroughs, with only one town, Bloomsburg, officially designated as such under state law.[[38]](#cite_note-38) They're originally based around a population center and in most cases correspond to the geographical designations used by the United States Census Bureau for reporting of housing and population statistics. Municipalities vary greatly in size, from the millions of residents of New York City and Los Angeles to the few hundred people who live in Jenkins, Minnesota.[[39]](#cite_note-39) In some instances, the term *town* refers to a small incorporated municipality of less than a population threshold specified by state statute, while in others a town can be significantly larger. Some states do not use the term *town* at all, while in others the term has no official meaning and is used informally to refer to a populated place, of any size, whether incorporated or unincorporated. In some other states, the words *town* and *city* are legally interchangeable. The [Census of Governments](/wiki/Census_of_Governments "Census of Governments") treats jurisdictions called towns in the [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England") states, [Minnesota](/wiki/Minnesota "Minnesota"), [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"), and [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin "Wisconsin") as [townships](/wiki/Civil_township "Civil township") rather than municipalities.[[40]](#cite_note-isd2022-40)

Small-town life has been a major theme in American literature, especially stories of rejection by young people leaving for the metropolis.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

Since the use of the term varies considerably by state, individual usages are presented in the following sections:

#### Alabama

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=57 "Edit section: Alabama")]

In [Alabama](/wiki/Alabama "Alabama"), the legal use of the terms *town* and *city* is based on population. A municipality with a population of 2,000 or more is a city, while less than 2,000 is a town (Code of Alabama 1975, [Section 11-40-6](https://web.archive.org/web/20130616104014/http://alisondb.legislature.state.al.us/acas/CodeOfAlabama/1975/11-40-6.htm)). For legislative purposes, municipalities are divided into eight classes based on population. Class 8 includes all towns, plus cities with populations of less than 6,000 (Code of Alabama 1975, [Section 11-40-12](https://web.archive.org/web/20130616104906/http://alisondb.legislature.state.al.us/acas/CodeOfAlabama/1975/11-40-12.htm)).

#### Arizona

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=58 "Edit section: Arizona")]

In [Arizona](/wiki/Arizona "Arizona"), the terms *town* and *city* are largely interchangeable. A community may incorporate under either a town or a city organization with no regard to population or other restrictions according to Arizona law (see [Arizona Revised Statutes](/wiki/Arizona_Revised_Statutes "Arizona Revised Statutes"), Title 9). Cities may function under slightly differing governmental systems, such as the option to organize a district system for city governments, but largely retain the same powers as towns. Arizona law also allows for the consolidation of neighboring towns and the unification of a city and a town, but makes no provision for the joining of two adjacent cities.

#### California

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=59 "Edit section: California")]

In [California](/wiki/California "California"), the words *town* and *city* are synonymous by law (see Cal. Govt. Code §§ 34500–34504).[[42]](#cite_note-42) There are two types of cities in California: charter and general law. Cities organized as charter cities derive their authority from a [charter](/wiki/Municipal_charter "Municipal charter") that they draft and file with the state, and which, among other things, states the municipality's name as "City of (Name)" or "Town of (Name)." Government Code §§ 34500–34504 apply to cities organized as [general law cities](/wiki/General-law_municipality "General-law municipality"), which differ from charter cities in that they do not have charters but instead operate with the powers conferred them by the pertinent sections of the Government Code. Like charter cities, general law cities may incorporate as "City of (Name)" or "Town of (Name)."

Some cities change what they are referred to as. The sign in front of the municipal offices in [Los Gatos, California](/wiki/Los_Gatos,_California "Los Gatos, California"), for example, reads "City of Los Gatos", but the words engraved on the building above the front entrance when the city hall was built read "Town of Los Gatos." There are also signs at the municipal corporation limit, some of which welcome visitors to the "City of Los Gatos" while older, adjacent signs welcome people to the "Town of Los Gatos." Meanwhile, the village does not exist in California as a municipal corporation. Instead, the word *town* is commonly used to indicate any unincorporated community that might otherwise be known as an unincorporated village. Additionally, some people may still use the word *town* as shorthand for *township*, which is not an incorporated municipality but an administrative division of a county.

#### Georgia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=60 "Edit section: Georgia")]

Georgia is divided into 159 [counties](/wiki/County_(United_States) "County (United States)") and contains 535 [municipalities](/wiki/Municipal_corporation "Municipal corporation") consisting of cities, towns, consolidated city-counties, and consolidated cities.[[43]](#cite_note-Census_Georgia_2010-43)[[44]](#cite_note-Census_2020-44) There is no legal difference in Georgia between cities and towns.[[45]](#cite_note-NGE-45)

#### Hawaii

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=61 "Edit section: Hawaii")]

In Hawaii, the Department of Business, Economic Development, and Tourism has the statutory authority to establish, modify, or abolish the statistical boundaries for cities, town, and. villages.[[46]](#cite_note-46) However, the only municipal government in Hawaii is the City and County of [Honolulu](/wiki/Honolulu "Honolulu").[[40]](#cite_note-isd2022-40)

#### Illinois

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=62 "Edit section: Illinois")]

In [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois "Illinois"), the word *town* has been used both to denote a subdivision of a county called a township,[[47]](#cite_note-47) and to denote a form of municipality similar to a [village](/wiki/Village "Village"), in that it is generally governed by a president and trustees rather than a mayor.[[48]](#cite_note-48) In some areas a town may be incorporated legally as a village (meaning it has at large trustees) or a city (meaning it has aldermen from districts) and absorb the duties of the township it is coterminous with (maintenance of birth records, certain welfare items). [Evanston](/wiki/Evanston,_Illinois "Evanston, Illinois"), [Berwyn](/wiki/Berwyn,_Illinois "Berwyn, Illinois") and [Cicero](/wiki/Cicero,_Illinois "Cicero, Illinois") are examples of towns in this manner. Under the current Illinois Municipal Code, an incorporated or unincorporated town may choose to incorporate as a city or as a village, but other forms of incorporation are no longer allowed.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

#### Indiana

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=63 "Edit section: Indiana")]

In [Indiana](/wiki/Indiana "Indiana"), a *town* is differentiated from a *city* in that a town can not become a city until it has a population of at least 2,000. The form of government is also different from that of a city in that the town council is both the legislative and executive branches of government. The mayor is selected by the council from within its ranks and operates as a [first among equals](/wiki/First_among_equals "First among equals").[[50]](#cite_note-50)

#### Louisiana

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=64 "Edit section: Louisiana")]

In [Louisiana](/wiki/Louisiana "Louisiana"), a town is defined as being a municipal government having a population of 1,001 to 4,999 inhabitants.[[40]](#cite_note-isd2022-40)

#### Maryland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=65 "Edit section: Maryland")]

While a town is generally considered a smaller entity than a city, the two terms are legally interchangeable in Maryland.[[40]](#cite_note-isd2022-40) The only exception is the [independent city](/wiki/Independent_city "Independent city") of [Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore "Baltimore"), which is a special case, as it was created by the [Constitution of Maryland](/wiki/Constitution_of_Maryland "Constitution of Maryland").

#### Mississippi

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=66 "Edit section: Mississippi")]

Municipalities in Mississippi are classified according to population size. At time of incorporation, municipalities with populations of more than 2,000 are classified as cities, municipalities containing between 301 and 2000 persons are classified as towns, and municipalities between 100 and 300 persons are classified as villages.[[51]](#cite_note-MississippiLaw-51) Places may be incorporated to become a city, town, or village through a petition signed by two-thirds of the qualified voters who reside in the proposed municipality.[[51]](#cite_note-MississippiLaw-51) The major function of municipal governments are to provide services for its citizens such as maintaining roads and bridges, providing law, fire protection, and health and sanitation services.[[51]](#cite_note-MississippiLaw-51)

#### Nevada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=67 "Edit section: Nevada")]

In [Nevada](/wiki/Nevada "Nevada"), a town has a form of government, but is not considered to be incorporated. It generally provides a limited range of services, such as [Land-use planning](/wiki/Land-use_planning "Land-use planning") and recreation, while leaving most services to the county. Many communities have found this "semi-incorporated" status attractive; the state has only 20 incorporated [cities](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Nevada "List of cities in Nevada"), and towns as large as [Paradise](/wiki/Paradise,_Nevada "Paradise, Nevada") (186,020 in 2000 Census), home of the [Las Vegas Strip](/wiki/Las_Vegas_Strip "Las Vegas Strip"). Most [county seats](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") are also towns, not cities.

#### New England

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=68 "Edit section: New England")]

Main article: [New England town](/wiki/New_England_town "New England town")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Downtown_Ipswich_MA.jpg/250px-Downtown_Ipswich_MA.jpg)](/wiki/File:Downtown_Ipswich_MA.jpg)

A downtown of [Ipswich, Massachusetts](/wiki/Ipswich,_Massachusetts "Ipswich, Massachusetts")

In the six [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England") states, a [town](/wiki/New_England_town "New England town") is the most prevalent minor civil division, and in most cases, are a more important form of government than the county. In [Connecticut](/wiki/Connecticut "Connecticut"), [Rhode Island](/wiki/Rhode_Island "Rhode Island") and seven out of fourteen counties in [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts"), in fact, counties only exist as boundaries for state services and chambers of commerce at most, and have no independent legal functions. In [New Hampshire](/wiki/New_Hampshire "New Hampshire"), [Maine](/wiki/Maine "Maine"), and [Vermont](/wiki/Vermont "Vermont"), counties function at a limited scope, and are still not as important in northern New England as they are outside of the northeast. In all six states, towns perform functions that in most states would be county functions. The defining feature of a New England town, as opposed to a city, is that a [town meeting](/wiki/Town_meeting "Town meeting") and a [board of selectmen](/wiki/Board_of_selectmen "Board of selectmen") serve as the main form of government for a town, while cities are run by a mayor and a city council. For example, [Brookline, Massachusetts](/wiki/Brookline,_Massachusetts "Brookline, Massachusetts") is a town, even though it is fairly urban, because of its form of government. In the three southern New England states, the entire land area is divided into towns and cities, while the three northern states have small areas that are unincorporated. In Vermont and New Hampshire, the population of these areas is practically nonexistent, while in Maine, unincorporated areas make up roughly half of the state's area but only one percent of the state's population.

Though the U.S. Census Bureau defines New England towns as "minor civil divisions" for statistical purposes, all New England towns are [municipal corporations](/wiki/Municipal_corporations "Municipal corporations") equivalent to cities in all legal respects, except for form of government. For statistical purposes, the Census Bureau uses [census-designated places](/wiki/Census-designated_place "Census-designated place") for the built-up population centers within towns, though these have no legal or social recognition for residents of those towns. Similarly, the Census Bureau uses a special designation for [urban areas](/wiki/Urban_areas "Urban areas") within New England, the [New England city and town area](/wiki/New_England_city_and_town_area "New England city and town area"), instead of the [metropolitan statistical area](/wiki/Metropolitan_statistical_area "Metropolitan statistical area") it uses in the rest of the country.

#### New Jersey

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=69 "Edit section: New Jersey")]

Main article: [Town (New Jersey)](/wiki/Town_(New_Jersey) "Town (New Jersey)")

A *town* in the context of New Jersey local government refers to one of five types and one of eleven forms of municipal government. While *town* is often used as a shorthand to refer to a township, the two are not the same. The Town Act of 1895 allowed any municipality or area with a population exceeding 5,000 to become a Town through a petition and referendum process. Under the 1895 Act, a newly incorporated town was divided into at least three wards, with two councilmen per ward serving staggered two-year terms, and one councilman at large, who also served a two-year term. The councilman at large served as chairman of the town council. The Town Act of 1988 completely revised the town form of government and applied to all towns incorporated under the Town Act of 1895 and to those incorporated by a special charter granted by the Legislature prior to 1875.

Under the 1988 Act, the mayor is also the councilman at large, serving a term of two years, unless increased to three years by a petition and referendum process. The council under the Town Act of 1988 consists of eight members serving staggered two-year terms with two elected from each of four wards. One council member from each ward is up for election each year. Towns with different structures predating the 1988 Act may retain those features unless changed by a petition and referendum process. Two new provisions were added in 1991 to the statutes governing towns. First, a petition and referendum process was created whereby the voters can require that the mayor and town council be elected to four-year terms of office. The second new provision defines the election procedure in towns with wards. The mayor in a town chairs the town council and heads the municipal government. The mayor may both vote on legislation before council and veto ordinances. A veto may be overridden by a vote of two-thirds of all the members of the council. The council may enact an ordinance to delegate all or a portion of the executive responsibilities of the town to a municipal administrator. Fifteen New Jersey municipalities currently have a type of town, nine of which operate under the town form of government.

#### New York

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=70 "Edit section: New York")]

Main article: [Administrative divisions of New York (state) § Town](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_New_York_(state)#Town "Administrative divisions of New York (state)")

In [New York](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_New_York "Administrative divisions of New York"), a town is a division of the county that possesses home rule powers, but generally with fewer functions than towns in New England. A town provides a closer level of governance than its enclosing county, providing almost all municipal services to unincorporated communities, called [hamlets](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_New_York_(state)#Hamlet "Administrative divisions of New York (state)"), and selected services to incorporated areas, called [villages](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_New_York_(state)#Village "Administrative divisions of New York (state)"). In New York, a town typically contains a number of such hamlets and villages. However, due to their independent nature, incorporated villages may exist in two towns or even two counties (example: [Almond (village), New York](/wiki/Almond_(village),_New_York "Almond (village), New York")). Everyone in New York who does not live in a city or [Indian](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") reservation lives in a town and possibly in one of the town's hamlets or villages. New York City and [Geneva](/wiki/Geneva,_New_York "Geneva, New York") are the only two cities that span county boundaries. The only part of Geneva in [Seneca County](/wiki/Seneca_County,_New_York "Seneca County, New York") is water; each of the [boroughs of New York City](/wiki/Boroughs_of_New_York_City "Boroughs of New York City") is a county.

#### North Carolina

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=71 "Edit section: North Carolina")]

In [North Carolina](/wiki/North_Carolina "North Carolina"), all cities, towns, and villages are incorporated as [municipalities](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_North_Carolina "List of municipalities in North Carolina"). According to the North Carolina League of Municipalities,[[52]](#cite_note-52) there is no legal distinction among a city, town, or village—it is a matter of preference of the local government. Some North Carolina cities have populations as small as 1,000 residents, while some towns, such as [Cary](/wiki/Cary,_North_Carolina "Cary, North Carolina"), have populations of greater than 100,000.

#### Oklahoma

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=72 "Edit section: Oklahoma")]

In [Oklahoma](/wiki/Oklahoma "Oklahoma"), according to the state's municipal code, *city* means a municipality which has incorporated as a city in accordance with the laws of the state, whereas *town* means a municipality which has incorporated as a town in accordance with the laws of the state, and *municipality* means any incorporated city or town.[[53]](#cite_note-OklahomaLaw1-53) The term *village* is not defined or used in the act.[[53]](#cite_note-OklahomaLaw1-53) Any community of people residing in compact form may become incorporated as a town; however, if the resident population is one thousand or more, a town or community of people residing in compact form may become incorporated as a city.[[54]](#cite_note-OklahomaLaw2-54)

#### Pennsylvania

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=73 "Edit section: Pennsylvania")]

In [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania"), the incorporated divisions are townships, boroughs, and cities, of which boroughs are equivalent to towns (example: [State College](/wiki/State_College,_Pennsylvania "State College, Pennsylvania") is a borough). However, one borough is incorporated as a town: [Bloomsburg](/wiki/Bloomsburg,_Pennsylvania "Bloomsburg, Pennsylvania").

#### South Carolina

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=74 "Edit section: South Carolina")]

At incorporation, municipalities may choose to be named either "City of" or "Town of", however there is no legal difference between the two.[[55]](#cite_note-55) All municipalities are responsible for providing local service including law enforcement, fire protection, waste and water management, planning and zoning, recreational facilities, and street lighting.[[56]](#cite_note-56) Municipalities may incorporate with one of three forms of government: 141 chose [mayor–council](/wiki/Mayor%E2%80%93council_government "Mayor–council government"), 95 chose [council](/wiki/Municipal_council "Municipal council"), and 33 chose [council–manager](/wiki/Council%E2%80%93manager_government "Council–manager government").[[57]](#cite_note-57)

#### Tennessee

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=75 "Edit section: Tennessee")]

Some Tennessee municipalities are called "cities" and others are called "towns."[[58]](#cite_note-58) These terms do not have legal significance in Tennessee[[59]](#cite_note-59) and are not related to population, date of establishment, or type of [municipal charter](/wiki/Municipal_charter "Municipal charter").

#### Texas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=76 "Edit section: Texas")]

In [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas"), although some municipalities refer to themselves as "towns" or "villages" (to market themselves as an attractive place to live), these names have no specific designation in Texas law; legally all incorporated places are considered [cities](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_Texas "List of municipalities in Texas").

#### Utah

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=77 "Edit section: Utah")]

See also: [List of municipalities in Utah](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_Utah "List of municipalities in Utah")

In [Utah](/wiki/Utah "Utah"), the legal use of the terms *town* and *city* is based on population. A municipality with a population of 1,000 or more is a city, while less than 1,000 is a town. In addition, cities are divided into five separate classes based on the population.[[60]](#cite_note-60)

#### Virginia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=78 "Edit section: Virginia")]

Main article: [Administrative divisions of Virginia § Towns](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_Virginia#Towns "Administrative divisions of Virginia")

In [Virginia](/wiki/Virginia "Virginia"), a town is an incorporated municipality similar to a city (though with a smaller required minimum population). But while cities are by Virginia law independent of counties, towns are contained within counties.[[61]](#cite_note-61)

#### Washington

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=79 "Edit section: Washington")]

Further information: [City government in Washington (state)](/wiki/City_government_in_Washington_(state) "City government in Washington (state)")

A town in the state of [Washington](/wiki/Washington_(state) "Washington (state)") is a municipality that has a population of less than 1,500 at incorporation, however an existing town can reorganize as a code city.[[62]](#cite_note-62) Town government authority is limited relative to [cities](/wiki/City_(Washington) "City (Washington)"), the other main classification of municipalities in the state.[[63]](#cite_note-63) As of 2012,[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit) most municipalities in Washington are cities (see: [List of towns in Washington](/wiki/List_of_towns_in_Washington "List of towns in Washington")).

#### Wisconsin

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=80 "Edit section: Wisconsin")]

Main article: [Administrative divisions of Wisconsin](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_Wisconsin "Administrative divisions of Wisconsin")

See also: [List of towns in Wisconsin](/wiki/List_of_towns_in_Wisconsin "List of towns in Wisconsin")

[Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin "Wisconsin") has towns which are areas outside of incorporated cities and villages. These towns retain the name of the civil township from which they evolved and are often the same name as a neighboring city. Some towns, especially those in urban areas, have services similar to those of incorporated cities, such as police departments. These towns will, from time to time, incorporate into cities, such as [Fox Crossing](/wiki/Fox_Crossing,_Wisconsin "Fox Crossing, Wisconsin") in 2016 from the former town of [Menasha](/wiki/Menasha_(town),_Wisconsin "Menasha (town), Wisconsin").[[64]](#cite_note-64) Often this is to avoid annexation into neighboring cities and villages.

#### Wyoming

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=81 "Edit section: Wyoming")]

A [Wyoming](/wiki/Wyoming "Wyoming") statute indicates towns are incorporated municipalities with populations of less than 4,000. Municipalities of 4,000 or more residents are considered "first-class cities".[[65]](#cite_note-65)
Some examples are [Moorcroft, Wyoming](/wiki/Moorcroft,_Wyoming "Moorcroft, Wyoming") and [Sundance, Wyoming](/wiki/Sundance,_Wyoming "Sundance, Wyoming").

### Vietnam

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Town&action=edit&section=82 "Edit section: Vietnam")]

In Vietnam, a [district-level town](/wiki/District-level_town_(Vietnam) "District-level town (Vietnam)") ([Vietnamese](/wiki/Vietnamese_language "Vietnamese language"): *thị xã*) is the second subdivision, below a province (*tỉnh*) or municipality (*thành phố trực thuộc trung ương*). A [commune-level town](/wiki/Commune-level_town_(Vietnam) "Commune-level town (Vietnam)") (*thị trấn*) a third-level (commune-level) subdivision, below a [district](/wiki/List_of_districts_of_Vietnam "List of districts of Vietnam") (*huyện*).

Archived from [the original](http://virginiaplaces.org/vagupnova/countytowncity.html) on 20 June 2010. Retrieved 31 May 2011. "Cities own and maintain their roads, while Virginia counties (except for Arlington and Henrico) rely upon [VDOT](/wiki/Virginia_Department_of_Transportation "Virginia Department of Transportation") for road maintenance. Cities get a fixed allocation of state funding for building and maintaining those roads, while counties must compete with each other and other VDOT priorities for a substantial portion of their road budget. Cities have been granted more authorities, such as the right of city councils to issue bonds to build roads without a voter referendum (counties must get voter approval in a referendum before issuing road bonds). In Virginia, towns have distinct boundaries, established by the [General Assembly](/wiki/Virginia_General_Assembly "Virginia General Assembly") or by courts guided by laws passed by the legislature. Towns are not independent from counties; residents of towns are still residents of the county in which the town is located. For example, residents of the four towns of [Haymarket](/wiki/Haymarket,_Virginia "Haymarket, Virginia"), [Quantico](/wiki/Quantico,_Virginia "Quantico, Virginia"), [Dumfries](/wiki/Dumfries,_Virginia "Dumfries, Virginia"), and [Occoquan](/wiki/Occoquan,_Virginia "Occoquan, Virginia") are also residents of [Prince William County](/wiki/Prince_William_County,_Virginia "Prince William County, Virginia"). They pay both town and county property taxes, and town residents get to vote for a town council/mayor."
